quote: I was just reaching out because it looks like we built up a solar design for you that we weren't able to go to quite yet. Does that sound familiar?
stewart_read: Parker's opener explicitly references a prior design build and prior contact — this is a recycled lead signal. The call never collected a utility bill, which looks like a collection gap. But on a recycled lead, Ion may already have Andy's bill on file from the prior touch. The coaching implication depends on whether the bill was already captured.
alternative_hypothesis: If this were a paid lead with no prior bill on file, skipping bill collection entirely would be a hard gap. On a recycled lead with a prior design already built, the bill may already be in the system.
coaching_implication: Before flagging bill collection as a miss on this call, the manager should verify whether Andy's bill was already captured in the prior engagement. If it was, Parker's skip is correct procedure. If it wasn't, this is a collection gap. Coach Parker to verbally confirm bill status at the top of recycled-lead calls: 'I still have your bill info from last time — does that still look right?' This surfaces the gap and builds credibility simultaneously.

quote: I will say just before you kinda dismiss it completely, I know solar in the past kinda wasn't an extra bill. And, to be honest with you though, now what we do is we do a bill swap. So instead of adding another bill to your plate, we're taking away your electric bill, and we're adding that solar bill, so we're swapping it.
stewart_read: This is the call's best moment. Parker correctly identified Andy's 'another bill' framing as an inversion opportunity and executed a bill-swap reframe without being prompted. The move is schema-aligned — it flips the cost-addition frame into a cost-replacement frame. Andy's 'Okay' at 02:13 confirms it landed. The execution is slightly soft ('kinda wasn't an extra bill' is imprecise) but the strategy is right.
alternative_hypothesis: One could read this as Parker reciting a memorized pitch rather than responding to Andy's specific financial anxiety. But the timing — immediately after Andy's 'another bill' language — and the direct mirroring of Andy's framing ('adding another bill to your plate') suggest Parker was responding to what he heard, not just running a script.
coaching_implication: Reinforce this move explicitly in the 1-on-1. Parker executed objection inversion correctly here. The coaching opportunity is sharpening the language: 'kinda wasn't an extra bill' undersells the flip. A tighter version would mirror Andy's exact words back before inverting them. No fabricated numbers needed — the emotional logic of 'swap, not add' is the leverage.

quote: So right now, we're probably looking at at least about five to six months down the road before we even can discuss anything like that.
stewart_read: Andy gives Parker an explicit timeline objection — five to six months — which is a flip opportunity. Parker's bill-swap reframe addressed the 'another bill' framing but never touched the timeline itself. The five-to-six-month deferral was bypassed, not resolved. This is a reactive gap: Parker moved to pitch without inverting the timeline.
alternative_hypothesis: One could argue Parker strategically chose not to fight the timeline and instead created enough curiosity with the bill-swap reframe to get Andy to self-select into a sooner appointment. The outcome (Tuesday booking) supports this read partially. But Parker never explicitly acknowledged or addressed the five-to-six-month frame, which leaves it as an unresolved objection the closer will likely encounter.
coaching_implication: Show Parker the gap between addressing 'another bill' and addressing 'five to six months.' These are two different objections. A flip on the timeline might sound like: 'The thing is, every month you wait is another month at your current rate — the design is already built, so the only thing waiting costs you is time at the higher number.' No fabricated savings figure needed — the logic of 'waiting has a cost' is the inversion. The closer should be briefed that this objection was bypassed, not resolved.

quote: No. It's just something I'm I'm gonna have to discuss with my my other half.
stewart_read: Andy raises the spouse signal clearly. Parker's intent appears inclusion-directed — at 03:47 he says 'we could call you and your partner' — but his execution never confirms the partner as a hard condition for the appointment. He uses calendar urgency ('our calendar does fill pretty quickly') to push toward booking rather than anchoring the partner's presence as the reason to book. The distinction matters: Parker's language frames the partner as someone Andy can 'double check with' after booking, not as a required participant whose availability should gate the time slot.
alternative_hypothesis: Parker's 03:47 'you and your partner' language suggests he intended to include the partner in the appointment. The strategic read is that he was threading inclusion into the booking rather than ignoring the protocol. This is the more supported hypothesis for intent — but the execution gap remains: Parker never asked 'What time works for both of you?' and never confirmed the partner would be present.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, distinguish intent from execution. Parker was moving toward inclusion — that's the right instinct. The gap is that he never locked it. The codex template is: 'What time works for both of you to be on the call together?' That question does two things: it confirms inclusion AND it makes the partner's availability the scheduling constraint, which protects the sit. Walk Parker through the difference between 'you can check with your partner' (soft, fragile) and 'what time works for both of you' (hard, protected).

quote: Alright. Why don't we do this? Is there any way you can email me and just so I can discuss it? I'm driving right now.
stewart_read: Andy asks for an email of the proposal/design. Parker holds the line correctly — 'we actually don't do just emails' — and pivots to a phone call appointment. This is schema-compliant execution of the hard rule. The 'I'm driving' context also explains Andy's channel preference and Parker correctly routes to a phone appointment rather than pushing for an in-person visit at this moment.
coaching_implication: This is a positive moment worth naming in the 1-on-1. Parker held the hard rule under mild pressure and offered a concrete alternative (phone call appointment) rather than just saying no. Reinforce the pattern: hold the rule, offer the path.

quote: Okay. I think we'll have our our specialist come by just because, you know, phone call is kind of our last resort. It it it works, but it's not nearly as good as having someone really come show it to you.
stewart_read: Andy explicitly offered both options ('Phone call or in person. Either way.') and Parker selected in-person. The channel choice itself is not a protocol violation — Andy consented. The issue is what Parker did not do after making the channel selection: no 'is that fair' handshake, no computer/no-driving anchor, no confirmation that the partner would be present, and no text follow-up promise. The button-up is structurally incomplete regardless of channel.
alternative_hypothesis: One could read Parker's in-person upgrade as a quality move — in-person sits convert better than phone calls, and Andy offered the option. This is the more supported read on the channel choice itself. The coaching gap is entirely in the missing button-up elements, not the channel selection.
coaching_implication: Separate the channel decision (correct) from the button-up execution (incomplete). Walk Parker through the required button-up elements per codex: appointment datetime confirmed, one-hour block stated, no-driving/at-computer anchor, 'is that fair' handshake, text follow-up promise. On this call, none of these were completed after the time was set. Each missing element is a no-show risk multiplier.

quote: Yeah. Well, I I mean, our calendar does fill pretty quickly. What I mean, I would love to just get you on the calendar before it fills up, and then you could double check with your, you know, your partner and see if it's a good time for you both.
stewart_read: Parker uses calendar urgency as a micro-trade to move past the spouse-check signal. The urgency framing ('our calendar does fill pretty quickly') is a legitimate tool, but the way it's deployed here frames the partner as an afterthought ('double check with your partner') rather than a required participant. This is the execution gap that makes the appointment fragile — the partner's availability was never confirmed as a condition of the booking.
alternative_hypothesis: The strategic read is that Parker used urgency to get Andy to commit to a time, intending to confirm the partner separately. But there's no evidence Parker planned a follow-up confirmation step, and the call ended without it.
coaching_implication: Calendar urgency is a valid tool. The coaching point is sequencing: urgency should follow partner-inclusion confirmation, not replace it. The correct order is: (1) 'What time works for both of you?' → (2) 'Our calendar fills fast, so let's lock that in now.' Parker reversed the sequence, which means the urgency landed but the inclusion didn't.

quote: Hey. Appreciate your time today, Andy. We'll talk to you on Tuesday.
stewart_read: Parker closes the call without completing any of the required button-up elements: no one-hour block stated, no 'is that fair' handshake, no computer/no-driving anchor, no text follow-up promise. The appointment is on the calendar but the verbal contract that protects the sit was never executed.
coaching_implication: Use this as a button-up checklist drill in the 1-on-1. Pull up the codex's required_elements list and walk through each one against this call. Parker got the time and day — that's the floor, not the ceiling. The 'is that fair' handshake alone is predictive of show rate. This is a repeatable close-of-call habit Parker needs to build, not a one-time miss.
